  1. It destroys communities: The Prophet ﷺ warned that the diseases of previous nations (ingratitude, arrogance, competing for worldly gains, ḥasad, hatred) would afflict the Muslim Ummah. When ḥasad spreads, communities fracture into factions that hate each other.

  2. It destroys scholarship and religious leadership: Those who know the religion - already a small minority of the Ummah - spend their energy fighting each other instead of serving the community. “This scholar is deviant,” “Don’t attend that conference because so-and-so will speak there,” “Our school of thought is right, everyone else is wrong.” The obsession with tearing others down leaves the masses unserved.

  3. It creates turf mentality: Religious work becomes about market share - “Don’t go to that masjid, come to our masjid,” “That organization is misguided, support our organization.” The Deen becomes divided into territories and tribes.

  4. It prevents accepting truth: When ḥasad exists between groups (sectarian, political, ideological), even when one side is correct about something, the other side cannot accept it. “If I agree with them on anything, people will think I’ve joined them.” This happened historically between Ahl al-Sunnah traditionalists and Mu’tazilah rationalists - possibly contributing to acceptance of problematic narrations just to oppose Mu’tazilah positions.

  5. It makes success punishable: In healthy societies, when someone fails, they receive support until they succeed. In societies infected with ḥasad, when someone succeeds, they are torn down until they fail. “We discourage our successes until they become failures.”

  6. It manifests in facial expressions and social interactions: The “evil eye” mentioned in hadith is not supernatural magic, but the reality that ḥasad shows in how people look at you, speak to you, gesture toward you. The Quran mentions the disbelievers’ hateful stares at the Prophet ﷺ : “وَإِن يَكَادُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصَارِهِمْ” (And indeed, those who disbelieve would almost make you slip with their eyes)(68:51).

The hadith that ḥasad “shaves away religion” explains the political dimension: when scholars, leaders, and activists are consumed with tearing each other down, the religion itself is shaved away from the community. The masses are left without guidance while those who should guide them are busy destroying one another.

The only cure the Quran offers for dealing with ḥasad directed at you is seeking Allah’s protection - because no human remedy exists. You cannot change the ḥāsid’s heart, you cannot reason with them, you cannot appease them. Only Allah ﷻ can protect you, and only Allah ﷻ can change their hearts if He wills.

Quranic Wisdom

Ayah 1-2

قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ الْفَلَقِ (1) مِن شَرِّ مَا خَلَقَ (2)

The fundamental wisdom is recognizing your absolute need for Allah’s protection at all times. Human beings have a tendency toward self-reliance and independence - “I can handle this myself” - but this surah teaches that true strength comes from acknowledging weakness before Allah ﷻ.

The daily recitation of this surah (as taught by the Prophet ﷺ to be recited in the morning, evening, and before sleep) trains the heart in constant awareness: I am never safe except through Allah’s protection. This awareness prevents spiritual arrogance and maintains humility.

Seeking refuge “in the Lord of Al-Falaq” (the One who tears things open) teaches that no difficulty is permanent, no darkness is unbreakable, no problem is unsolvable for Allah ﷻ. When you are in circumstances that seem impossible to escape - oppressive situations, overwhelming darkness, trapped by enemies or circumstances - remember that Allah ﷻ tears open the darkest night to bring morning. He can tear open your difficulty just as easily.

The recognition that everything created contains potential for good and evil teaches mindfulness about how you use Allah’s blessings. Your intelligence, your wealth, your social status, your abilities - all can be used to serve Allah ﷻ or to disobey Him. This awareness should make you constantly seek Allah’s guidance in using His blessings properly.

Ayah 3

وَمِن شَرِّ غَاسِقٍ إِذَا وَقَبَ

The spiritual lesson of darkness “when it intensifies” is that sometimes Allah ﷻ allows you to descend into very dark circumstances - seemingly hopeless situations - to teach you that only He can save you. When you are in the depths of darkness and it seems there is no way out (وَقَبَ - absorbed into a bottomless hole), that is precisely when you learn what it means to truly seek refuge in رَبِّ الْفَلَقِ - the Master who tears things open.

The metaphorical darkness includes spiritual darkness: being surrounded by people who mock your faith, workplaces where you are the only Muslim, families where no one supports your practice of Islam, environments saturated with sin. In these situations, the wisdom is to remember that Allah ﷻ can tear open these circumstances and bring you into light - whether by changing your circumstances or by giving you internal light (iman and guidance) that illuminates your path despite external darkness.

Practically, the mention of night teaches caution and wisdom: the same neighborhood safe during day becomes dangerous at night. Activities permissible during day become problematic at night. This is not superstition but wisdom based on human nature and shaytanic influence. Young people who argue “What’s wrong with being out at night?” are ignoring this divine wisdom.

Ayah 4

وَمِن شَرِّ ٱلنَّفَّـٰثَـٰتِ فِى ٱلْعُقَدِ

The warning against magic and superstitious practices contains deep wisdom about how shaytanic deception works. When people turn away from Allah as الصَّمَد (the fulfiller of all needs), they become vulnerable to nonsensical practices that promise supernatural solutions.

The wisdom for modern Muslims is to recognize and reject the countless ways magic has been dressed in Islamic garments: amulets with Quranic verses, “blessed” chocolates, water that has been “recited over,” numerological practices (“recite this 313 times”), fortune-telling disguised as Islamic consultation, etc.

True Islamic practice when facing difficulties: (1) Understand and reflect on the Quran’s message, (2) Let that understanding strengthen your iman and trust in Allah, (3) Make sincere dua to Allah, (4) Take appropriate worldly means (see a doctor for illness, work hard for provision, etc.), (5) Be patient and trust Allah’s plan.

The Prophet’s ﷺ example when visiting the sick companion teaches the proper balance: he recognized the problem (heart condition), made dua, and sent for a skilled doctor. He did not simply blow on the person or recite Quran and expect magical healing. The Quran’s healing power is spiritual - it heals the heart by strengthening iman, increasing hope in Allah ﷻ, bringing peace through remembrance of Allah ﷻ.

The deeper wisdom is that Allah’s religion is based on intelligence, reason, and common sense. When someone blows on knots, does this make rational sense? No. When someone claims to know the unseen, does this align with Quranic teaching? No - only Allah ﷻ knows the unseen. Islam came to liberate people from superstition and irrationality, not to promote it.

Ayah 5

وَمِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ

The placement of ḥasad as the final evil - mentioned after darkness and magic but with less dramatic language - teaches that the greatest dangers are often the least obvious. Physical threats are visible and can be avoided. Magical practices are obviously false to those with knowledge. But ḥasad lives invisibly in hearts and manifests in ways that are difficult to detect or defend against.

The wisdom for the individual Muslim is self-examination: Do I feel joy when something bad happens to someone else? Do I feel upset when something good happens to them? Do I want to see certain people fail even though their failure does not benefit me? Am I consumed with thoughts about someone else’s success or attention or blessings?

Recognizing ḥasad in yourself is the first step to removing it. The hadith mentions that ḥasad “shaves” the religion - but since it shaves rather than removes, it can grow back when you eliminate ḥasad from your heart. The cure for ḥasad in your own heart is gratitude for your own blessings and remembering that Allah’s distribution is based on His perfect wisdom.

The wisdom for dealing with others’ ḥasad toward you: (1) Recognize you cannot change their hearts - only Allah ﷻ can, (2) Maintain sabr and taqwa despite their plots or hatred, (3) Focus on Allah’s protection rather than obsessing about what they might do, (4) Remember that their ḥasad harms them more than it harms you - it shaves away their religion, (5) Continue your path of serving Allah ﷻ regardless of their attempts to bring you down.

The verse teaches that ḥasad only becomes truly harmful “إِذَا حَسَدَ” (when it manifests in action). As long as it remains in the heart, it primarily harms the one who harbors it. But when it leads to actions - backbiting, plotting, sabotage, spreading rumors, undermining others - then you need Allah’s protection from its effects.

The broader wisdom for the Ummah: Communities and nations fall into decline when ḥasad spreads among them. The Prophet ﷺ explicitly warned that ḥasad, hatred, competition for worldly gain, and arrogance destroyed previous nations and would afflict this Ummah. When religious leaders are consumed with attacking each other instead of serving their communities, when Muslims compete to tear each other down rather than building each other up, when success is punished rather than celebrated - this is the disease of ḥasad destroying the Ummah from within.

The ultimate wisdom of this entire surah is that your only real protection from any evil - external or internal, physical or spiritual, obvious or hidden - is Allah ﷻ. When you truly internalize this, you stop fearing people and circumstances, and you start fearing only Allah’s displeasure. When you hold firmly to Allah’s refuge, nothing else can truly harm you.

The surah’s connection to Surah Al-Ikhlas teaches: Because Allah is Al-Samad (the One all creation turns to for every need), and because your greatest need is protection, therefore turn to Him alone for refuge. Do not turn to amulets, magicians, fortune-tellers, superstitious practices, or your own strength. Turn only to Allah ﷻ, hold onto Him for dear life, and trust that the One who tears open the darkest dawn can tear open any darkness in your life.
